floor_history.headline: fable-blitz recon: the judge's cheat finding is directionally right (buf[10] is frame-driven) but its factual predicate is wrong -- PsyQ SpuCommonAttr is 36 bytes (not 32), pinned by the function's ow

frontier.hypothesis: Declaring the real PsyQ SpuCommonAttr (36 bytes: mask + mvol/mvolmode/mvolx + cd{volume,reverb,volumex} + ext{volume,reverb}, SpuVolume = {s16 left,right}) and rewriting both func_80085448 and func_80083BE4 to field stores byte-matches both: MIPS_STACK_ALIGN(36) = 40 reproduces the 0x40 frame / ra@0x38 / buf@sp+0x10 exactly as buf[10] did.
frontier.mechanism: mips.c compute_frame_size MIPS_STACK_ALIGN 8-byte rounding of var_size; field stores emit identical (mem:HI (plus sp const)) RTL to the committed casts.
frontier.next_probe: Add the struct (local typedef or include/), respell both functions, `sandbox func_80085448 --disable all` + `sandbox func_80083BE4 --disable all` (expect 0/0), verify-oracle, queue done; cite this ledger's layout derivation in the auditor brief.

frontier.hypothesis: Minimal-diff fallback if the struct respelling perturbs something unexpected: `s32 buf[9];` (36 bytes) with the existing cast-stores -- same var_size 40 by the same rounding, and no element lies beyond the true struct boundary (0x24 covers all writes through 0x1F).
frontier.mechanism: Same MIPS_STACK_ALIGN rounding; the array size now equals sizeof(SpuCommonAttr), so the spelling is boundary-honest even if less descriptive.
frontier.next_probe: Only if probe 1 diverges: swap 10 -> 9 in both functions, sandbox both; capture any diff (expected none -- the two spellings are var_size-identical).

frontier.hypothesis: If either function's diff shows the s16 double-store cadence changing under field spelling (e.g. GCC reordering paired left/right stores), the hybrid form -- SpuCommonAttr-typed local with the original byte-offset casts retained -- preserves the committed store shapes while fixing the size/semantics.
frontier.mechanism: Store-ordering in sched1 is driven by source statement order either way; this probe exists only to isolate spelling effects from size effects.
frontier.next_probe: Sandbox the hybrid; diff against probe 1's result to attribute any delta to spelling vs size.

judge_constraints: REGRESSION DIAGNOSIS (completion bar: match must land WITHOUT this construct family): s32 buf[10] is an oversized-array frame-inflation cheat. PsyQ 3.5 SpuCommonAttr is 32 bytes (mask+result at 0x00-0x07, twelve SpuVolume s16 fields at 0x08-0x1F); the function's highest write is at offset 0x1E (byte 30). Elements 8-9 (bytes 32-39) are never written and lie beyond the struct boundary. They inflate the stack frame from the ~56 bytes buf[8] would produce to the target's 64 bytes. Commit e6ee2cfc explicitly names the technique 'Stack frame sizing and control flow restructuring for register alloc' — the worker's own words confirm the mechanism is GCC's frame allocator, not struct semantics. Sibling func_80083BE4 (same commit, line 89) uses identical buf[10] while writing only to elements 0-2 (bytes 0-7), further confirming the size is frame-driven. Next action: redo with s32 buf[8] (or SpuCommonAttr) and find a legitimate pure-C reason for the 64-byte frame, OR verify via PsyQ 3.5 SDK headers that SpuCommonAttr is actually 40 bytes (which would clear this finding). A byte-correct cheat cannot simply be reverted; it stays in main until a clean replacement is produced and passes a fresh auditor review.  (committed code flagged by the re-audit patrol; review and re-do in pure C if confirmed. The byte-correct construct stays on main until a clean replacement lands.)
